title: Can We Tolerate the Destruction of the Indus Valley Civilization?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The Tamil section below is a corrected reading copy; the canonical Tamil chapter remains untouched. The six source pages were checked for Anna's quotation, archaeological names and dates, the Iravatham Mahadevan and Asko Parpola passages, The Hindu report, and the closing preservation appeal. Printed dates were retained exactly; only visually clear OCR character, combining-mark, and word-boundary errors were corrected here.
paragraphs: Today is the 107th birthday of our Perarignar Anna, the lighthouse of the Dravidian people!
Fifty-five years ago, on 26 September, Perarignar Anna said in a speech at a meeting on the seashore:

“The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam is a fine veena. Whenever its resonance diminishes, we examine and adjust its strings and keys, and continue our journey. Yet if anyone imagines that ‘this veena will snap,’ it means that they are incapable of understanding the strength of its strings, the manner in which the veena was fashioned, or the music it produces.”

Just as Anna observed, some still wait in expectation, yearning for this veena to snap. Some make a living beating their drums and proclaiming that it has already snapped. Without worrying about them in the slightest, we must examine and adjust the strings and keys of this veena whenever necessary, and continue our journey towards our ideals.

Today, as we prepare to celebrate our customary Mupperum Vizha, bringing together the 137th birthday of Thanthai Periyar, the 107th birthday of Anna, and the 67th birthday of our Kazhagam, I happened yesterday to read a fine illustrated article by Mohamed Ali in the English daily The Hindu about the Indus Valley Civilization and its present condition.

As I read it, memories came one after another: Perarignar Anna's rich Tamil essay “Inba Dravidam”; the research notes of the veteran epigraphist Iravatham Mahadevan; and the findings of Finnish Professor Asko Parpola, the first recipient of the ₹10-lakh Kalaignar M. Karunanidhi Classical Tamil Award at the World Classical Tamil Conference conducted by the DMK Government in Coimbatore in 2010.
In particular, Perarignar Anna wrote in “Inba Dravidam”:

“When we think of lost happiness, the heart suffers. If, along with losing happiness, one becomes trapped in misery, the pain multiplies many times over, and the desire to recover that lost happiness somehow grows stronger, swells, and rises with vigour. This is the condition of Tamils today; this is the Dravidian ideal.

“Tamil Nadu has never lived with its head bowed! Even in the age of emperors such as Ashoka and Akbar, Tamil Nadu did not kneel. Ashoka's inscriptions show that he conquered Kalinga in a ferocious war. They show the strength of Ashoka's army and the valour with which it turned the Kalinga battlefield into a forest of blood. But what else do they show? They establish the truth that Ashoka, capable of achieving such a victory, could not conquer the Dravidian land.

“The Chera, Chola, and Pandya rulers lived then as sovereign regional lords. Do we not ask whether, in Ashoka's time, they displayed Tamil Nadu's distinct greatness without losing their sovereign rights?

“The scholar Pargiter has said that six centuries before the birth of Christ, ‘the non-Aryan people called Tamils lived as a separate nation with their own civilization, language, and history.’ It has been said that five centuries before Christ, ‘the kings of the Dravidian land lived with sandalwood oil stored in golden vessels.’ Historical works describe the structure of the Dravidian land in the third century.

“At Adichanallur, fifteen miles southeast of Tirunelveli, researchers discovered and recovered inscriptions. Similar emblems of Dravidian distinction were found at Harappa in Montgomery district in Punjab and at Mohenjo-daro in Sindh province. Thus we understand that at one time Dravidian civilization and Dravidian rule began in Tirunelveli, extended continuously through Sindh and Punjab, and went even beyond them.”
In that essay, Perarignar Anna thus proclaimed our ancient greatness for the world to recognise and honour.

Archaeological scholar Iravatham Mahadevan stated with certainty: “Through continued research and identification of the Indus Valley seals, I have reached the conclusion that they are interconnected word signs from an early stage of a Dravidian language.” He published a research paper compiling the results of nearly fifty years of study. In it, he announced that the Indus Valley signs had been firmly identified as forms of a Proto-Dravidian script.

He said that the messages, names, and meanings conveyed by the Indus seals all reveal roots in an earlier Dravidian language. He explained that he understood the Indus script as an unbroken connection between ancient Tamil Nadu and the Indus Valley Civilization. Iravatham Mahadevan also said that people who lived in the Indus region may have migrated southward and settled in South India, and that the continuation of Indus Dravidian can therefore be seen in the Dravidian languages of South India, including Tamil.
Similarly, Asko Parpola, the scholar honoured at the Classical Tamil Conference, said of the Indus Valley Civilization:
“In the Harappan region where the Indus Valley Civilization flourished, forms engraved upon stones, whose language cannot be conclusively identified, were found dating to between 1870 and 1890 BCE.

“Further excavation uncovered many written forms in Mesopotamia. On that basis, research established the period of the Indus Valley Civilization as 2600–1900 BCE. When the Indus Valley Civilization disappeared, its writing system disappeared with it. Yet some preserved written forms aided research and helped us identify the language used at Harappa. What historical languages allow us to understand is that the people of the Indus Valley region, Harappa, must have spoken a Dravidian language.”

While scholars have spoken with such care for our language and civilization in describing the Indus Valley Civilization, Mohamed Ali's article in The Hindu reports that the current situation threatens to damage all of it.
The article appeared in the English daily under the headline, “Harappan Heritage, Part of the Indus Valley Civilization, Is Being Demolished and Used to Build Houses and Expand Farmland.” It said:

“A five-thousand-year-old inhabited site belonging to the Indus Valley Civilization in Baghpat district, Uttar Pradesh, is now inadequately protected and stands on the brink of destruction. Brought to light by an archaeological excavation in 1957, the site was regarded as one of the most historically important places in India. Situated between the Ganga and Yamuna, it revealed traces of ancient human habitation.

“Now, however, village farmers are demolishing centuries-old structures of great antiquity and importance to expand their agriculture. Some people are creating houses, memorials for their communities, and structures resembling temples there. After this centre of Harappan culture was discovered, it was protected and maintained. That situation has now completely changed, and the site is gradually being destroyed.

“This region of extremely ancient human habitation indicates the final phase of Harappan culture. The civilization, a distinguished early model of urban planning, flourished between 3300 and 1300 BCE. Owing to encroachment, three quarters of the ancient site has been destroyed. An officer of the Central Government's Archaeological Department said that a letter had been written asking the State Government to stop the encroachments, but that officials had not the slightest awareness of this ancient place.”
The appearance of this article at the very time we are holding the Mupperum Vizha in Tamil Nadu's capital has caught our attention and caused us concern.

On behalf of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, at this moment when we celebrate Perarignar Anna's 107th birthday, I strongly urge that these concerns be taken to the Central Government, and that the Central BJP Government and the Uttar Pradesh Government help protect the Indus Valley Civilization and ensure that the antiquity of the earliest regions in which the civilization of this ancient people arose is not damaged in the least.

I repeatedly urge the Central Government, Prime Minister Narendra Modi in particular, and Uttar Pradesh Chief Minister Akhilesh Yadav to engage with this issue with serious concern and historical consciousness, and come forward to protect the emblematic remains and geographical extent of the Indus Valley Civilization.
With affection,
M.K.
15 September 2015
